Kevin, you must be shopping three or four hours a day, which is not unusual.
Industry statistics show that people shop anywhere from four to six sites before they buy a ticket.
And, Kevin is obviously a classic example there.
You think of all the (unintelligible) you'd get if you'd find a good price.
Oh, yeah.
Now, I mean, there's definitely - there's - it's a game basically.
People love to find - it's a hobby sometimes to find the best deal.
Let me give you a little bit of background on your question.
Kayak.com actually hits a variety of systems to pull in prices.
They don't actually hit Travelocity or Expedia.

They actually get it from a variety of airlines and a couple of it, and they do a great job of aggregating that information and bringing it to the fore so you can see multiple airlines, because there can be price differences.
AirFareWatchdog is another site that likes to do what I call deal-finding, which is they have a group of people that go out, and, you know, kind of try to fare it out.

Our big claim to fame at FareCompare.com is that we get airfare directly from the airlines, and we can post alerts, which is the biggest thing that you can do right now is to know when prices drop before they even hit the airline Web sites, because our technology actually process the data in about five minutes, and we send out those alerts eight times a day as opposed to a lot of those companies that send out alerts only at 2 o'clock or 3 o'clock in the morning.
And a lot of times, those deals are only good for partial days.
So, it's really important to know when prices change.
Also, as a…
You know, (unintelligible) or something.
Yeah.
Well, basically I like to think of buying airfare sort of like the Kelley Blue Book or Edmunds.
You need to know what a good price is first.

I think one of the biggest challenges consumers have is they go and they try Rome, Italy, Europe, and they try it for June, they try it for July, they try it for May, and they don't know necessarily what's a good deal and what's a bad deal.
And then, a lot of times when they do see a good deal, they run home to their spouse to get approval and the deal is done by the time they get home.
So you got to be willing to pull the trigger as well when you see a good deal.
So not only do you have to know you have a good deal, you have to be able to pull the trigger quickly nowadays.
And another thing that's happening right now is a program called Clear Skies.
This is supposed to make life cheaper and a lot easier for people traveling to Europe.
Is that going to change things quickly?
Oh, yeah.
I think you're probably referring to Open Skies.
Open Skies, excuse me.
Yes.
Yeah.

Open Skies is definitely - it just started this Sunday, March 30th, and it basically allows free market competition between the European community and the U.S., whereas before it was very difficult for airlines to get flights into certain places like Heathrow.
What you're seeing just this week, you'll see, like, six or seven new airlines launching a bunch of new flights all at sale prices.
And hopefully, what we'll see in the next year is several of the lower-cost airlines starting to fly transatlantic, which would really drive the price point down.
Right now, you know, we're looking at prices, you know, where the base airfare is $150 each way to London all the way up until May, even from New York.
That's because the fuel surcharge is at 2.15 and taxes and fees are another $115 or so.
We're at a record low for actual airfare cost, it's just that the price of fuel has really driven up the cost.
And, of course, once you get there, the currency conversion is a little bit more difficult.
Actually in 2007, there were more inbound foreign travelers into the U.S.
from international than any other year before, and they were all bringing empty suitcases, taking stuff home with them.

Well, there's a couple strategy that I'd like to do, especially when getting people over to Asia.

One thing is that you can spend the night in Los Angeles somewhere, get a low-cost airline into Los Angeles; relax, because you're in for a long flight anyway, maybe spend the night there, do whatever.
And then, you can get a lot cheaper flights out of the L.A.
area into Asia.
Sometimes, in the San Francisco area, they have some good flights and some inexpensive flights.
And then second of all, if you - the time you want to go to really minimize your cost is right at the end of May.
Usually, that breakpoint this year is going to occur around the weekend of May 24th, which is a Saturday.
Mm-hmm.
And that's when the prices jump up very seriously.
And this also occurs in Europe as well.
Now, every airline has a little bit different season.
One might be a week before, one might be a week after, but that's really going to be your best bet.
